frozen_components: pre-trained 7 billion parameter autoregressive transformer language model
architecture.summary: Frozen trains a vision encoder (NF-ResNet-50) to map images into continuous embeddings matching a pre-trained frozen language model's token embedding space. The vision encoder outputs are linearly projected and reshaped into a sequence of 2 visual prefix embeddings. During training, image-caption pairs from Conceptual Captions are used; the language model weights remain frozen while backpropagation flows through its self-attention layers to optimize the encoder. At inference, images and text can be interleaved arbitrarily in the prompt, enabling the frozen language model to perform multimodal tasks via in-context learning.
trained_components: linear projection layer (ResNet output to visual prefix embeddings)

biblio.title: Multimodal Few-Shot Learning with Frozen Language Models

claims.statement: On VQAv2, zero-shot transfer from captioning training achieves 29.5% accuracy; with 1-shot in-context learning 35.7%; with 4-shot 38.2%.
On OKVQA (requiring outside knowledge), the 7B parameter model achieves 12.5% zero-shot accuracy and 19.6% with VQAv2 training data mixed in.

On Open-Ended miniImageNet 2-way with task induction and 5 inner-shots, Frozen achieves 58.9% accuracy; performance scales with number of distinct exemplars (inner-shots) more than repetitions of single exemplars.
On Open-Ended miniImageNet 5-way, Frozen achieves only 22.3% accuracy with 3 inner-shots and task induction, not significantly above 20% chance baseline.
On Fast-VQA (binding new words to visual categories), Frozen improves from 1.6% with 0 shots to 7.9% with 5 shots; blind baseline remains near 0.4%.
Keeping the language model frozen generalizes substantially better to visual question-answering than fine-tuning the language model weights.

A model trained from scratch end-to-end shows no transfer at all from captioning to VQA, suggesting that the generalization abilities of large language models rely upon large-scale text-only training in which predicting the next token mimics the test setting.
Frozen achieves far from state-of-the-art performance on specific tasks it learns in a few shots compared to systems using the full training set for those tasks.
